notes: The login form at GET /login (HTTP 200) submits to POST /session with fields 'identifier' (email) and 'secret' (password).
Both admin@example.com/password and user@example.com/password also returned HTTP 200 with session=ok, consistent with the broken auth check.
The regression is that credential validation is bypassed for any non-empty input: the app sets session=ok regardless of whether the supplied identifier/secret match any real account.

observations.value: POST /session with completely invalid credentials (identifier=wrong@wrong.com, secret=badpassword) returns HTTP 200, sets 'Set-Cookie: session=ok; Path=/', and renders the authenticated Home page ('Welcome. Sign out'). Authentication is not actually validating credentials — any non-empty identifier/secret pair is accepted as a successful login.

POST /session with empty identifier and empty secret returns HTTP 401 with body containing 'Invalid credentials.' — confirming the server does have a rejection path, but it only triggers on empty inputs, not on genuinely wrong credentials.

detections.observation_text: POST /session with completely invalid credentials (identifier=wrong@wrong.com, secret=badpassword) returns HTTP 200, sets 'Set-Cookie: session=ok; Path=/', and renders the authenticated Home page ('Welcome. Sign out'). Authentication is not actually validating credentials — any non-empty identifier/secret pair is accepted as a successful login.

POST /session with empty identifier and empty secret returns HTTP 401 with body containing 'Invalid credentials.' — confirming the server does have a rejection path, but it only triggers on empty inputs, not on genuinely wrong credentials.
